Dear Ms. Arnento:
We have received your letter dated November 19, 1990, seeking
written confinnation of tetephone advice previously provided to
you by this agency. You had sought advice on behalf of tlro mem-
bers of the city of south San Francisco city council- who hold
California real estate licenses. Your inquiry concerned whether
the conflict-of- interest provisions of the PoIitical Reform Act
(the 'tAct") 1 required the councilmembers to disqualify thensel,ves
from participating in decisions before the council, sitting as the
city's redevelopment agency, that involve the city's redevelopment
areas.
Your query does not involve a pending governmental decision.
we are thus treating your request, and the confirmation provided,
as informal advice.2 The advice is prospective in its application
and is lirnited only to the provisions of the Act; as you know, we
do not provide advice $rith respect to prior conduct, previous de-
cisions, or other conf l ict-of- interest statutes (such as Govern-
nent code Section l-090).
We confirm that on the basis of the facts you provided in the
prior telephone inquiry, and reiterated in your letter of November
19, 1990, a disqualifying conflict of interest does not arise in
decisions before the councj.I invotving the city's redevelopnent
areas simply by virtue of the possession of a real estate license.

tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 3 22-5660

This advice is prernised on your representation that neither
councj,lmember is actively engaged, nor has current plans to en-
gage, in real estate transactions within the jurisdiction- 
You
ieie advised that disqualif icatj.on fron partici-pating in a par-
ticular future decision would depend on the facts pertinent to the
decision and the relationship between the councilmember's license
and other activities with the decision itself.
You were advised that the effect of a decision is reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial tikelihood that it will
occur. whil,e certainty is not required, an effect that is rnerely
a possibility or otherwise simply speculative is not reasonably
foieseeable. (Do$rnev cares v. Downev communitv DeveloDment com.
(1987) 195 Ca1.App.3d 983i rn re Thorner (L975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
The comnission has prevj,ousty concluded, in examinj-ng a re-
development issue in terms of a councilmenber's business as a real
estate broker active in a city's redevelopment area, that the pro-
posed redevelopment would naterially financially inpact the coun-
iiLmember's rell estate business because the business earned its
income fron comnissions based on a percentage of the value of the
property within the area. (In re oqlesbv (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)
For this reason, you were advised previously (Arnento Advice
Letter, No. A-90-499) that another menber of the city council had
a disqualifying conflict as the owner of a erell-established real
estat6 complny which did 80? of its business in the city.
In the telephone advice provided you' you were informed that
the possession oi a real estate license alone was insufficient to
co.,=Litute a disqualifyinq conflict of interest. 
While the real
estate business may be directed related to redevelopnent actj-vity
(Arnento Advice Letter, suDra) , the advice that you lrere provided,
and which we confirm in this tetter, is prenised on the fact that
the two counc i lroernbers are not involved in any real estate busi-
ness. As indicated above, our advice could change should one or
both councilmernbers begin to make active use of their licenses and
become involved in the real estate business in the jurisdiction'
I hope this letter has provided you with the gnridance you
requested-. Please contact ne if you have any further questions

sections.question: Whether the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act require two members of the City of South San Francisco City Council who hold California real estate licenses to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions before the council, sitting as the city's redevelopment agency, that involve the city's redevelopment areas.
sections.conclusion: We confirm that on the basis of the facts you provided in the prior telephone inquiry, and reiterated in your letter of November 19, 1990, a disqualifying conflict of interest does not arise in decisions before the council involving the city's redevelopment areas simply by virtue of the possession of a real estate license.
sections.question_synthetic: Do city council members holding real estate licenses have a disqualifying conflict of interest when voting on redevelopment decisions simply by virtue of possessing a real estate license?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A disqualifying conflict of interest does not arise from merely holding a real estate license when voting on redevelopment matters, provided the council members are not actively engaged in real estate transactions within the jurisdiction. However, disqualification requirements could change if the council members begin actively using their licenses and engaging in real estate business activities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a confirmation of prior telephone advice formatted as an advice letter. Includes detailed legal analysis and citations to prior FPPC opinions. Advice is prospective and limited to Political Reform Act provisions only; does not provide immunity. Key condition: advice assumes council members are not actively engaged in real estate transactions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act require two members of the City of South San Francisco City Council who hold California real estate licenses to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions before the council, sitting as the city's redevelopment agency, that involve the city's redevelopment areas.

CONCLUSION: We confirm that on the basis of the facts you provided in the prior telephone inquiry, and reiterated in your letter of November 19, 1990, a disqualifying conflict of interest does not arise in decisions before the council involving the city's redevelopment areas simply by virtue of the possession of a real estate license.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms informal telephone advice that city council members holding California real estate licenses do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when voting on redevelopment decisions, provided they are not actively engaged in real estate business activities within the jurisdiction.
